QF.id: XIII Trading, Clearing, Settlement and Risk Management_20

question.text: How does the position limit structure for interest rate derivatives differ from equity derivatives?

answer.text: IR derivatives use notional value-based limits (e.g., Rs. X crore), while equity derivatives primarily use MWPL (percentage of free-float shares) for individual stocks
Both use identical percentage-of-open-interest based limits
IR derivatives have no position limits while equity derivatives have strict limits
Equity derivatives use notional limits while IR derivatives use MWPL

explanation.text: **The Logic:** IR derivatives position limits are typically specified in terms of notional value of bonds (e.g., specific lot and position limits per entity). Equity stock derivatives use MWPL (20% of free-float shares). Index derivatives use separate OI-based limits. Currency derivatives use USD-denominated limits. Each segment has its own structure. **The Trap:** Every segment has position limits — IR is not exempt. The limits are structured differently based on the nature of the underlying. **Cross-Exam Context:** This is a XIII-unique cross-asset comparison. Series I (USD-based limits), IV (notional value), VIII (MWPL/OI percentage) each have distinct position limit frameworks. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always check the specific position limit structure for the derivative segment you're trading in — exceeding limits results in penalties and forced closure of positions.
